message.content: {"schema_version":"stella.literature_hvs_candidates.v0.1","generated_at":"2026-06-13T01:09:50","paper":{"arxiv_id":"1901.04559","bibcode":"2019ApJ...872...58H","title":"Identifying Sagittarius Stream Stars By Their APOGEE Chemical Abundance Signatures","month":"2019-01","source_note_json":"notes/2019/2019-01/2019-01.json","links":{"abs":"https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.04559","pdf":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.04559"}},"inputs":{"paper_dir":"literature/1901.04559","audit_path":"literature/1901.04559/audit.json","catalog_review_path":"literature/1901.04559/catalog_review.json","catalog_extraction_path":"literature/1901.04559/catalog_extraction.json","ecsv_paths":[]},"extraction":{"status":"partial","extracted_at":"2026-06-13T01:09:50","extractor":"agent","summary":"Paper identifies one hypervelocity star candidate (HVS) that is unbound in MWPotential2014. The HVS is selected from the Sgr chemical candidate cluster and found to be unbound based on orbit integration. The APOGEE ID of the HVS is not available in the provided arxiv source (the full Table 1 is omitted).","tooling":null},"method_chain":[{"id":"step-01","depends_on":[],"step_type":"input_catalog","summary":"APOGEE DR14 data release, including stellar parameters, chemical abundances, distances from the value-added catalog, and radial velocities, covering ~176,000 red giant stars.","inputs":["APOGEE DR14 catalog"],"outputs":["APOGEE stellar sample with Teff, logg, [Fe/H], chemical abundances, distances, radial velocities"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":160,"end_line":164,"context":"description of APOGEE sample and DR14"}]},{"id":"step-02","depends_on":[],"step_type":"input_catalog","summary":"Gaia DR2 data for proper motions.","inputs":["Gaia DR2"],"outputs":["proper motion catalog"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":178,"end_line":180,"context":"Gaia DR2 proper motions acquisition"}]},{"id":"step-03","depends_on":["step-01"],"step_type":"quality_filter","summary":"Apply quality cuts to APOGEE sample for chemical clustering: Teff between 3550 and 4200 K, S/N >70, no ASPCAPBAD flag, [Fe/H] > -1.2.","inputs":["APOGEE DR14 sample"],"outputs":["Clean sample of cool red giants"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":165,"end_line":175,"context":"sample definition for clustering"}]},{"id":"step-04","depends_on":["step-03"],"step_type":"sample_selection","summary":"Perform k-means clustering (k=16) in 6-dimensional chemical abundance space ([O/Fe], [(C+N)/Fe], [Mg/Fe], [Al/Fe], [Mn/Fe], [Ni/Fe]) to identify stars with Sgr-like abundance patterns, referred to as Sgr stream candidates. The HVS candidate is one of these chemically selected stars.","inputs":["Chemical abundances of the clean sample"],"outputs":["68 Sgr stream candidates"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":219,"end_line":248,"context":"k-means clustering method and selection of Sgr stream candidates"}]},{"id":"step-05","depends_on":["step-01","step-02"],"step_type":"cross_match","summary":"Cross-match APOGEE stars with Gaia DR2 using nearest-neighbor within 1 arcsec in TOPCAT, obtaining Gaia proper motions for 62 of 68 candidates.","inputs":["APOGEE catalog","Gaia DR2"],"outputs":["Proper motions for Sgr stream candidates"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":179,"end_line":181,"context":"Gaia cross-match"}]},{"id":"step-06","depends_on":["step-01"],"step_type":"distance_estimation","summary":"Adopt spectro-photometric distances from the APOGEE DR14 value-added catalog (NMSU_dist), accurate to ~20%.","inputs":["APOGEE DR14 VAC distances"],"outputs":["distances"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":175,"end_line":176,"context":"distance estimates from DR14 VAC"}]},{"id":"step-07","depends_on":["step-01"],"step_type":"radial_velocity_measurement","summary":"Use radial velocities from APOGEE DR14 spectra as provided by the ASPCAP pipeline.","inputs":["APOGEE spectra"],"outputs":["radial velocities"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":160,"end_line":163,"context":"APOGEE data includes radial velocities"}]},{"id":"step-08","depends_on":[],"step_type":"solar_position_and_motion","summary":"Adopt solar Galactocentric distance R0 = 8.0 kpc, circular velocity Vcirc = 220 km/s, and solar peculiar motion from Schönrich et al. (2010).","parameters":[{"name":"R0","raw_value":"8.0 kpc","value":"8.0","unit":"kpc","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":182,"end_line":182,"context":"solar radius 8.0 kpc"}]},{"name":"v_circ_sun","raw_value":"220 km/s","value":"220","unit":"km s^-1","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":182,"end_line":182,"context":"circular velocity 220 km/s"}]},{"name":"solar_motion_u","raw_value":"as measured by Schönrich+2010","value":"","description":"U component from Schönrich+2010","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":182,"end_line":182,"context":"solar motion from Schönrich+2010"}]}],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":181,"end_line":182,"context":"adopted solar position and motion"}]},{"id":"step-09","depends_on":["step-05","step-06","step-07","step-08"],"step_type":"velocity_calculation","summary":"Calculate Galactocentric Cartesian velocities (U, V, W) using Gaia proper motions, distances, radial velocities, and the solar parameters, following a left-handed coordinate system with X toward Galactic center, Y along rotation, Z toward north Galactic pole.","inputs":["proper motions","distances","radial velocities","solar parameters"],"outputs":["Galactocentric velocities (U,V,W) and total space velocity"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":181,"end_line":182,"context":"description of kinematic calculations and coordinate system"}]},{"id":"step-10","depends_on":[],"step_type":"galactic_potential_model","summary":"Use the MWPotential2014 gravitational potential model, a composite of a spheroidal bulge, Miyamoto-Nagai disk, and NFW halo, as implemented in galpy.","parameters":[{"name":"potential_name","raw_value":"MWPotential2014","value":"MWPotential2014","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":181,"end_line":181,"context":"MWPotential2014"}]}],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":181,"end_line":181,"context":"usage of MWPotential2014 from galpy"}]},{"id":"step-11","depends_on":["step-09","step-10"],"step_type":"orbit_integration","summary":"Integrate orbits of the Sgr stream candidates forward and backward for 1.5 Gyr using galpy in MWPotential2014, deriving orbital parameters including eccentricity, apocenter, pericenter, angular momentum.","inputs":["Galactocentric positions and velocities","MWPotential2014"],"outputs":["orbital parameters (eccentricity, rap, rperi, ...)"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":181,"end_line":182,"context":"orbit integration description"}]},{"id":"step-12","depends_on":["step-11","step-10"],"step_type":"escape_or_bound_assessment","summary":"Assess whether each star is bound or unbound. The HVS candidate is found to have no measured apocenter (at the last time step it is ~760 kpc from the Galactic center) and is therefore unbound in MWPotential2014.","inputs":["orbit integration results","MWPotential2014"],"outputs":["bound/unbound status"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":296,"end_line":298,"context":"HVS: no measured apocenter, unbound"},{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":389,"end_line":391,"context":"HVS orbit details and unbound conclusion"}]},{"id":"step-13","depends_on":["step-12"],"step_type":"candidate_classification","summary":"Classify candidates into groups: Sgr stream, accreted halo, bulge, HVS, others. The HVS is identified as a separate category based on its unbound orbit.","inputs":["orbit parameters and bound status"],"outputs":["classification labels"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":290,"end_line":298,"context":"definition of groups including HVS"}]}],"candidates":[{"identifiers":{"record_id":"1901.04559:cand-001","paper_candidate_id":"HVS candidate","gaia_source_id":"","all":[{"value":"HVS candidate","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":296,"end_line":298,"context":"introducing HVS group"}]},{"value":"d","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":321,"end_line":321,"context":"table footnote: d = HVS"}]}]}}],"candidate_groups_considered":[{"group_id":"table-1","description":"Table 1: Sgr Stream Cluster, listing APOGEE IDs with membership assignments (a-e), including the HVS candidate (d).","decision":"included","reason":"The HVS candidate (membership code d) is identified in this table; it is the only object with Galactic unbound status.","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":304,"end_line":325,"context":"caption and partial table of Sgr Stream Cluster"}]}]}
message.reasoning_content:
We need to fill the skeleton for paper 1901.04559. We have the skeleton already provided.

Let's review inputs:

- catalog_review.json: mentions "62 stars ... one hypervelocity star candidate". So there is an HVS candidate. Also mentions "paper mentions finding a hypervelocity star". It does not give any tables extracted: catalog_extraction.json shows no tables (skipped). So we don't have ECSV tables.

- arxiv_source directory: we have the .tex file (trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex) and .bbl file. The paper text includes discussion of a HVS candidate.

We need to fill:

- extraction: status, summary, tooling? Tooling fields are empty in skeleton; we can leave empty but must fill if we know them. The skill says: "Fill `extraction.tooling` with the real instrument provenance of the run that writes the file: `agent_runtime` (agent platform or pipeline name and version), `model_id` (the exact dated model identifier actually serving this run), `prompt_version` (the git commit or tag of this skill text), and `request_parameters` (for example temperature) when known. Never guess these values or copy them from examples; batch pipelines fill them programmatically from the API request/response. `--require-complete` rejects files whose `model_id` or `prompt_version` is empty."

We are in a simulated environment. We don't have actual tooling info. However we are supposed to complete the skeleton. Since the user message says "You work without tools: every input file you are allowed to use is included verbatim in the user message; you cannot open files or browse." So we cannot get actual tooling info. The skeleton had empty tooling. We might need to fill with something? The skill says: "Fill `extraction.tooling` with the real instrument provenance ... Never guess ... batch pipelines fill them programmatically". For this stage, maybe we should leave tooling as empty or with placeholder? But validation might require non-empty for --require-complete. The skeleton has empty agent_runtime, model_id, prompt_version, request_parameters. I think we should not guess; we can leave empty but validation might warn. The instruction: "Return ONLY the requested JSON ... no markdown fences, no commentary." We'll output the JSON with empty tooling as in skeleton, or maybe we should fill with "unknown"? The schema says ToolingMeta has optional fields? The schema shows: "tooling": { "anyOf": [ { "$ref": "#/$defs/ToolingMeta" }, { "type": "null" } ], "default": null }. So tooling can be null. And the skeleton has null. But later when we fill extraction, we need to set tooling maybe as null. The skill says: "Fill `extraction.tooling` with the real instrument provenance ...". This suggests we should eventually fill it. But for now, we are just completing the scaffold and roster. The final validation step will be run after full completion. Since we only need to provide the scaffold with identifiers roster, we can leave tooling as null.

We need to produce the complete candidate roster: list of candidates. The paper says: "one hypervelocity star candidate." In the results, they list groups: Sgr Stream (35), Accreted Halo (20), Bulge (4), HVS (1 star), Others (2). So the HVS is one star. There might be only one HVS candidate. However the inclusion criteria includes only stars treated as possibly unbound from the Milky Way. The paper describes the HVS: "its observed velocities suggest it is unbound in MWPotential2014" and "completely unbound from the Milky Way." So it's definitely a candidate. The other groups are not claimed to be unbound; they are Sgr stream stars, accreted halo, bulge, others, all likely bound. So only one candidate.

Thus we need candidates[] with one entry, containing identifiers for that star. We need to get its paper candidate ID: The paper doesn't explicitly name a separate ID for the HVS; it just describes "One star in the Sgr chemical sample". In Table \ref{tab:stream_cand}, they list APOGEE IDs and membership: d = HVS. So the HVS is identified by its APOGEE ID. The table caption: "Membership* a = Sgr stream, b = accreted halo, c = bulge, d = HVS, e = others". We need to extract the APOGEE ID for the HVS. The table is in the .tex source, but we only have the .tex file (the whole thing). The table is shown with lines 304-325. The table lists several rows, but only the first few lines, then "..." and a line "The full list is available from the online journal." So the table as shown does not show the HVS row. We need to find the full list? But the arxiv source doesn't include the full table, only a stub. So we don't have the HVS APOGEE ID from the provided source. However, the paper mentions "one star ..." and did not give an explicit name. However, in the .tex, later they might refer to the HVS with some data? The paper text in Section 4.4 "HVS" discusses the HVS candidate but doesn't give its APOGEE ID. It gives some details: "This star's Gaia proper motions are (\mu_{\alpha\cos\delta}, \mu_\delta) = (-1.91, -0.10) \pm (0.62, 0.44) mas yr^{-1}". That might be used to identify the star, but we can't cross-match without external data. The paper's catalog extraction is empty, so we don't have an ECSV table with APOGEE IDs. So we are stuck.

But the skill says: "If the paper does not state the RA/Dec frame or epoch and the survey/data release is absent from that reference, do not guess; set the nested RA/Dec reference_frame.value and epoch.value to unknown with inference_basis ... preserve the paper-visible catalog or table evidence." That's about RA/Dec. But for candidate identifiers, we need paper_candidate_id. We could assign a placeholder, but we need the paper's own identifier. The paper uses APOGEE ID like "2M00151324-1430281". The HVS should have an APOGEE ID. Since we don't have the full table, we cannot extract it. However, we might infer from other parts of the paper: maybe the paper mentions the APOGEE ID in the text? Let's scan the .tex file for the HVS discussion. Around line 389: "One star in the Sgr chemical sample is at a Galactocentric distance of ~54 kpc ..." It doesn't state APOGEE ID. It mentions proper motions, but no ID. So we cannot get the ID.

We need to look at the .bbl file; maybe there are references but not helpful. So we cannot provide a paper_candidate_id based solely on available source. The stored arxiv_source is only the .tex and .bbl; the full table is missing. So we are missing data. But the skill says: "If ADS API metadata was not fetched, ... explicit tell the user which paper is affected and why before continuing." But we have the ADS metadata bibcode already? The skeleton has bibcode "2019ApJ...872...58H". So that's fine. The missing data is the full table. That may be in an online journal, but we don't have it. So we might need to report that the table is incomplete, and we cannot extract the HVS identifier. The skill says: "If ... any required archive asset is unavailable, explicitly tell the user which paper is affected and why before continuing." In this simulated pipeline, the user expects us to output the JSON? The instruction: "Return ONLY the requested JSON — no markdown fences, no commentary." So we cannot in the output tell the user. So we should perhaps set the candidate roster empty and note in extraction summary that the table is missing? But we are supposed to fill candidate roster. The skill says: "Do not silently drop missing 6D components; leave the field absent and mention the gap in inclusion_assessment or extraction.summary." So maybe we can still create a candidate entry with identifiers that are empty for paper_candidate_id? But the schema requires paper_candidate_id: string, with property "paper_candidate_id" required. That cannot be empty string? The schema: "paper_candidate_id": {"title": "Paper Candidate Id", "type": "string"} is required. So we must provide a non-empty string. So we need something like "APOGEE ID"? But we don't know it.

We could try to find the APOGEE ID by searching the .tex file for "d" entries in the table. However, the table is truncated with "...**". The full list is not in the .tex. So we can't.

Perhaps the HVS is actually mentioned in the text with its APOGEE ID somewhere else? Let's search the .tex for "2M" (the APOGEE ID prefix). In the table stub, we see lines: "2M00151324-1430281", "2M00194682-1345014", etc. But those belong to Sgr stream (membership a). The HVS (d) is not listed. I see no other 2M IDs elsewhere in the text. So we cannot extract.

Given the limitation, we could still produce a candidate entry with a synthetic paper_candidate_id like "HVS-1" but that's not paper-given. The paper uses APOGEE IDs as identifiers. The paper would refer to the HVS by its APOGEE ID if they'd list it. Since we can't, we might need to mark extraction status as "needs_review" and mention missing table. But the task: "Complete the skeleton EXCEPT candidate details. Fill extraction (status, summary), the full method_chain, and candidate_groups_considered exactly per the skill. For candidates, return an EXHAUSTIVE roster of identifier stubs: one entry per object the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way, each containing ONLY the identifiers object (record_id, paper_candidate_id, gaia_source_id, all[] with source_refs). Do not include any other candidate fields yet. The roster must be complete even if there are hundreds of objects — never sample, truncate, or pick representatives; keep extraction.summary consistent with the roster you actually list." This is Stage 1: we need to produce the roster. We cannot include candidate details yet. But we must include identifiers for each candidate. If we cannot extract the identifier, we might need to note that the HVS is present but its APOGEE ID is unknown. The skill says: "For candidates, return an EXHAUSTIVE roster of identifier stubs: one entry per object the paper treats as possibly unbound ...". So we should list one candidate. The paper_candidate_id must be provided. Perhaps we can use "HVS" as a placeholder? But that's not paper-visible identifier. The paper might refer to it as "HVS" or "hypervelocity star candidate". The text says: "We also find one hypervelocity star candidate." So the paper's label is "hypervelocity star candidate". Could we use that as paper_candidate_id? The schema field "paper_candidate_id" expects the paper display name. The skill says: "the chosen display identifier in identifiers.paper_candidate_id". It could be a descriptive string like "hypervelocity star candidate". That might be acceptable? But typically it would be a specific ID from the paper. The paper doesn't assign a specific name, but it's clearly a distinguishable object. I think it's acceptable to use "HVS candidate" or "hypervelocity star candidate" as paper_candidate_id. The paper's text says "one hypervelocity star candidate". So we can use that. The identifiers.all[] can contain that string plus maybe "d" from the table code. But we don't have the APOGEE ID. So we'll list the paper_candidate_id as "hypervelocity star candidate". And all[] can include that. We'll set gaia_source_id to empty string.

We also need to include source_refs for the paper_candidate_id: reference the text lines where the HVS is mentioned. So we can cite lines 296-298 where the HVS is introduced: "We also find one hypervelocity star candidate." That's line 296-298? Actually lines: "  \item HVS (1 star) --- This star does not appear on this plot because it has no measured apocenter ...". That's line 296. And later the HVS section (lines 389-403). So we can reference those.

Thus we can produce a candidate roster with one entry.

Now fill the extraction: status should be "candidates_found" because we have candidates. The extraction summary can describe: "Paper identifies one hypervelocity star candidate (HVS) that is unbound in MWPotential2014. Inclusion based on explicit text claiming unbound status. The APOGEE ID of the HVS is not available in the provided arxiv source (full table omitted)." Since we can't fill detailed data, that's okay for the summary. The status should be "candidates_found" (since we have candidates). The skeleton had "needs_review". We'll change to "candidates_found". But note that the table is incomplete, so maybe we set status to "needs_review" and mention missing table. The skill says status enum: "candidates_found", "no_candidates", "partial", "needs_review", "source_missing". I think "partial" or "needs_review" might be appropriate because we lack the APOGEE ID. But the candidate itself is identified. "source_missing" might fit if we can't fully extract. However, we have enough to identify it's a HVS candidate, just not the full identifier. I'll set status to "partial" and note the missing table. The summary can mention that the full table with APOGEE IDs is not in the arxiv source, so we cannot provide the APOGEE ID. I'll use "partial". But the skill says: "If ADS API metadata was not fetched, ... explicitly tell the user ...". The table missing is not ADS API. We'll still note it.

The extraction.tooling we'll leave as null for now (since we don't have real tooling info). The skeleton already has null.

Now fill method_chain: We need to derive the paper's method chain for identifying HVS candidate? Wait, the method_chain describes the overall paper method for selecting candidates, including kinematic selection, chemical clustering, orbit integration, etc. The paper's primary goal is to identify Sgr stream candidates chemically, but along the way they find an HVS. The method chain should reflect the full analysis pipeline used by the paper, as described in sections 2-5. The skill says: "Extract a paper-level atomic method_chain[] DAG: ... Use local step-01, step-02, ... IDs in paper order. Use only canonical step_type values from the generated schema reference. Each step must have depends_on listing only its direct upstream steps. Do not combine distance, velocity, orbit, and escape/bound assessment in one broad step." So we need to build the chain covering the steps that lead to the identification of the HVS, including input catalog, cross-matching, quality cuts, distance estimation, radial-velocity measurement, solar position and motion assumptions, velocity calculation, Galactic potential assumptions, orbit integration, escape/bound assessment. The paper uses APOGEE data for chemical abundances, distances, radial velocities; Gaia DR2 for proper motions; they integrate orbits in MWPotential2014 with galpy, using solar parameters (R0 = 8.0 kpc, Vcirc = 220 km/s, solar motion from Schönrich+2010). So we can define steps.

Let's list steps according to paper:

- Input catalogs: APOGEE DR14 (data release 14) with derived stellar parameters and chemical abundances. Also they supplement with Gaia DR2 proper motions (cross-matched). So we can have step for input_catalog: APOGEE DR14, and input_catalog: Gaia DR2. But cross-match is separate step. The schema allows input_catalog step type.

- Quality cuts: They apply cuts: Teff 3550-4200, S/N>70, no ASPCAPBAD flag, [Fe/H] > -1.2, distances d>5 kpc, color (J-K)_0 > 0.8, E(B-V)<0.75, proper motion limits. Also S/N>30 for the kinematic candidate selection. This is a sample_selection step.

- Cross-match with Gaia DR2: They use TOPCAT nearest neighbor within 1" to find Gaia proper motions for APOGEE stars. So cross_match step.

- Distance estimation: They adopt spectro-photometric distances from DR14 VAC (NMSU_dist), accurate to ~20%. So distance_estimation step.

- Radial velocity: from APOGEE spectra. So radial_velocity_measurement step (implicitly done by APOGEE pipeline). Could be input_catalog but paper directly uses APOGEE radial velocities. So we can have a step: radial_velocity_measurement: radial velocities from APOGEE DR14. But actually APOGEE provides radial velocities as part of pipeline; they didn't re-measure themselves. So step_type could be input_catalog as well, but we need to reflect that they obtain radial velocities from APOGEE. We'll create a step: radial_velocity_measurement, but summary says "APOGEE DR14 provides radial velocities from the ASPCAP pipeline." That's fine.

- Solar position and motion: they assume R0=8.0 kpc, Vcirc=220 km/s, solar motion from Schönrich+2010. So solar_position_and_motion step with parameters.

- Velocity calculation: They compute Galactocentric Cartesian velocities (U, V, W) from proper motions, distances, and radial velocities, using the solar parameters. So velocity_calculation step.

- Galactic potential model: They use MWPotential2014 (or for comparison, Law&Majewski2010 potential). So galactic_potential_model step.

- Orbit integration: They integrate orbits forward and backward for 1.5 Gyr using galpy. So orbit_integration step.

- Escape/bound assessment: They assess whether star is unbound in MWPotential2014 based on orbit integration (star's orbit shows it's unbound). So escape_or_bound_assessment step.

- Candidate classification: they classify stars into groups, including HVS. So candidate_classification step (maybe after orbit integration). But the HVS group is determined by having no measured apocenter (i.e., unbound). So escape/bound assessment leads to HVS classification. So we can include step: candidate_classification after escape/bound assessment.

We also need to include the chemical clustering steps? That's part of selecting Sgr stream candidates, but the HVS is selected from the Sgr chemical cluster, so the method chain should include the clustering step. However, the HVS was identified from the Sgr chemical sample (which was selected via k-means clustering). Then its orbit was integrated and found unbound. So the pipeline for HVS includes the chemical selection, which is a candidate_classification or sample_selection step that uses chemical abundances to select Sgr stream candidates. So we should include a step: "sample_selection: k-means clustering on 6 chemical abundance ratios to identify Sgr-like stars" which produces the candidate list including HVS. Then the orbit integration and bound assessment.

Thus full chain:

step-01: input_catalog: APOGEE DR14
step-02: input_catalog: Gaia DR2
step-03: quality_filter: apply Teff, S/N, ASPCAPBAD, etc.
step-04: sample_selection: kinematically selected Sgr stream candidates (for control) and chemical clustering sample selection (applying d>5, Beta<20, etc.)? Actually the paper uses a separate kinematic control sample selection for validation, but the HVS is not directly from that. But the overall sample on which clustering is performed includes the HVS. So step-03 quality_filter applies to the APOGEE sample to define the "MW sample" for clustering (the sample with Teff, S/N, etc., and Fe/H > -1.2). Then they also apply additional cuts to define the search sample (d>5, Beta<20, etc.)? The clustering analysis is performed on the MW sample with those parameter cuts, but they also later impose the additional cuts for the candidate selection? I think the clustering was done on the full MW sample with those parameter cuts, and then the Sgr stream candidates are drawn from that cluster. Then they further select 68 stars as Sgr chemical candidates. The HVS is one of those. So we need to represent that. So step-03: quality_filter (applying Teff, S/N, ASPCAPBAD, Fe/H > -1.2). That yields the "MW sample" for clustering. Then step-04: sample_selection (k-means clustering to select Sgr stream candidates). Then step-05: cross_match (cross-match with Gaia DR2 for proper motions). Then step-06: distance_estimation (use DR14 VAC distances). step-07: radial_velocity_measurement (APOGEE RVs). step-08: solar_position_and_motion. step-09: velocity_calculation. step-10: galactic_potential_model. step-11: orbit_integration. step-12: escape_or_bound_assessment. step-13: candidate_classification (classify as HVS, Sgr stream, etc.).

The depends_on relations:
- step-02 depends on none (input).
- step-03 depends on step-01 (input).

Now construct method_chain array with steps.

Step IDs: step-01, step-02, etc.

We'll create steps as:

1. step-01: input_catalog: APOGEE DR14
2. step-02: input_catalog: Gaia DR2
3. step-03: quality_filter: apply quality cuts to APOGEE sample
4. step-04: sample_selection: k-means clustering in chemical abundance space to select Sgr stream candidates
5. step-05: cross_match: cross-match APOGEE with Gaia DR2 using TOPCAT
6. step-06: distance_estimation: adopt spectro-photometric distances from DR14 VAC
7. step-07: radial_velocity_measurement: adopt APOGEE radial velocities
8. step-08: solar_position_and_motion: adopt R0=8.0 kpc, Vcirc=220 km/s, solar motion from Schönrich+2010
9. step-09: velocity_calculation: compute Galactocentric Cartesian velocities (U,V,W) from proper motions, distances, RVs, and solar parameters.
10. step-10: galactic_potential_model: use MWPotential2014
11. step-11: orbit_integration: integrate orbits using galpy in MWPotential2014
12. step-12: escape_or_bound_assessment: determine whether star is unbound based on orbit (apocenter not reached, velocity exceeds escape)
13. step-13: candidate_classification: classify stars into groups (Sgr stream, accreted halo, bulge, HVS, others)
